methodology_text:
The Z-Prospect Model

Overview: Fantasy football is a game about numbers. We care about production. So if we can nail down the most important inputs to that production, we'll be long-term winners. The goal of the prospect models is to predict how well a running back and wide receiver will perform in fantasy football across his first three seasons in the league. To measure performance, the model averages out a player's best two seasons in points per game (in point per reception leagues) across the first three years of his career. This is referred to as a player's B2S (Best Two Seasons) average. The model also incorporates draft capital, since the model needed to beat the correlation between draft capital and B2S to be worthwhile. With the help of draft capital, both the wide receiver and running back models predicted a player's best two seasons across his first three years way better than draft capital alone. The Z-Prospect Model performed better than fantasy public ADP — significantly better.

The Inputs — Running Backs: The running back model produces a size score, which is based on a player's Body Mass Index and weight. The model also utilizes Speed Score, a weight-adjusted 40-yard dash time. A Speed Score threshold of 90 needs to be hit or else points are deducted. Next, there's age — older running backs entering the league get dinged more than someone who's just 20 or 21. Finally, there are three main production metrics that the model utilizes to formulate a stat score: best-season reception share, best-season total touchdown share, and best-season total yards per team play. Add in draft capital, and we're cookin'.

The Inputs — Wide Receivers: The wide receiver model has a little more to it. Age matters a lot more at wide receiver. Breakout Age (the age when a college wide receiver hits a Dominator Rating of 20% or greater) and early-declare status are key inputs. Size is an input but not hugely important. NFL Combine athleticism measurables don't matter for wide receivers — it's likely already captured in draft capital. Three production statistics: best-season receptions per game, best-season receiving yards per team pass attempt, and best-season touchdown share. Receiving yards per team pass attempt gets the most weight. The final two inputs are conference factor (wideouts who played in specific conferences get additional points) and teammate factor (receivers who played with good teammates get a slight bump).

The Outputs: The model is tested against historical data. Wide receivers ranking in the 80th to 90th percentile have fared similarly to players in the 90th to 95th. There's a very clear drop-off when a player is unable to hit the 80th percentile as a prospect. At running back, focus is on the 85th percentile or better.

Draft Capital Delta: The percentile difference between a player's Z-Prospect Model score and draft capital percentile. Players with higher Draft Capital Deltas will become values more often than players with lower ones. Of drafted RBs with Draft Capital Delta above 5% and three years in the league, 22.6% hit a double-digit B2S; among those at -5% or worse, less than 7%. The same trend holds at WR (15.4% vs 9.0%).

Adding Subjectivity: Seeing how a player looks in a model can be helpful, but watching the player play is always a solid idea as well. The models are a foundation; nuance and context matter (e.g., injuries, scheme fit, PFF grades, return yardage).

Positional Focus: The Z-Prospect Model only covers running backs and wide receivers. Quarterback and tight end models are in development but not ready. For tight ends: 'Grab athletic tight ends who get drafted early.' Post-draft adds a Post-Draft Rankings Cheatsheet with tiered rankings for single-QB and Superflex formats, including QBs and TEs.
